title: PowerShell passes objects not text
question: How does the PowerShell pipeline differ fundamentally from traditional shells like Bash?
answer: In PowerShell the pipeline passes rich .NET objects between commands, not plain text. Each command emits structured objects with properties and methods, so downstream cmdlets can access typed data directly without parsing strings. This eliminates fragile text-scraping that is common in Unix shells.
explanation: Traditional shells stream bytes/text, forcing tools like awk, sed, and cut to re-parse output. PowerShell instead moves live objects, so a property like LastWriteTime is a real DateTime you can compare, sort, or format. Text is only rendered at the very end when output is displayed to the host.
codeExample:
# Objects flow through the pipeline, not text
Get-Process |
    Where-Object { $_.CPU -gt 100 } |
    Sort-Object CPU -Descending |
    Select-Object Name, CPU, Id

# $_ .CPU is a real [double], not a substring of text
bestPractices: Work with object properties instead of parsing text
Defer formatting to the end of the pipeline
commonMistakes: Treating cmdlet output as strings and using text tools
interviewTips: Contrast with Bash text streams to show understanding

title: Verb-Noun cmdlet naming
question: What is the verb-noun naming convention for cmdlets and why does it matter?
answer: Cmdlets follow a Verb-Noun pattern such as Get-Process or Set-Item, where the verb comes from an approved verb list and the noun is singular. This consistency makes commands discoverable and predictable, so once you know the pattern you can guess command names and use Get-Command to find them.
explanation: Approved verbs (viewable with Get-Verb) group actions into categories like Common, Data, and Lifecycle. Using non-standard verbs triggers analyzer warnings and hurts discoverability. The singular noun keeps naming uniform even when a command returns many objects.
codeExample:
Get-Verb | Select-Object Verb, Group | Sort-Object Group

# Discover commands by noun
Get-Command -Noun Process

# Discover commands by verb
Get-Command -Verb Get -Module Microsoft.PowerShell.Management
bestPractices: Use approved verbs from Get-Verb
Keep nouns singular
commonMistakes: Inventing custom verbs like Create- or Delete-
interviewTips: Mention Get-Verb to show tooling awareness

title: Inspecting objects with Get-Member
question: How do you discover the properties and methods of an object in PowerShell?
answer: Pipe the object to Get-Member (alias gm) to list its type name, properties, methods, and events. This is the primary discovery tool for exploring unfamiliar output and understanding what data and actions are available on an object.
explanation: Get-Member reflects over the .NET type of each object in the pipeline. You can filter by MemberType to see only properties or only methods. It is essential because the default formatted view often hides many available properties.
codeExample:
Get-Process | Get-Member

# Only show properties
Get-Service | Get-Member -MemberType Property

# Only show methods
(Get-Item .).psobject.Methods | Select-Object Name
bestPractices: Use Get-Member to explore before scripting against output
commonMistakes: Assuming displayed columns are the only properties
interviewTips: Pair Get-Member with Get-Command and Get-Help as the discovery trio

title: Select, Where, and ForEach-Object
question: What are the roles of Select-Object, Where-Object, and ForEach-Object in the pipeline?
answer: Where-Object filters objects by a condition, Select-Object chooses or projects properties (and can limit count), and ForEach-Object runs a script block against each object. Together they form the core of pipeline data shaping: filter rows, pick columns, and transform items.
explanation: Where-Object emits only objects for which its script block returns true. Select-Object can pick properties, create calculated properties, or take -First/-Last. ForEach-Object iterates and can execute arbitrary code, optionally with Begin/Process/End blocks for streaming work.
codeExample:
Get-ChildItem -File |
    Where-Object { $_.Length -gt 1MB } |
    ForEach-Object { $_.Name.ToUpper() } |
    Select-Object -First 5

# Calculated property
Get-Process | Select-Object Name, @{ Name = 'MB'; Expression = { [math]::Round($_.WS / 1MB, 2) } }
bestPractices: Filter early with Where-Object to reduce pipeline volume
Use calculated properties for on-the-fly projection
commonMistakes: Confusing Select-Object (columns) with Where-Object (rows)
interviewTips: Show a calculated property to demonstrate depth

title: Automatic variables $_, $?, $PSItem
question: What are the automatic variables $_, $PSItem, and $? used for?
answer: $_ and its full name $PSItem represent the current object in a pipeline script block, such as inside Where-Object or ForEach-Object. $? is a boolean holding whether the last command succeeded. These are maintained by PowerShell automatically rather than assigned by the user.
explanation: $_ / $PSItem let you reference the piped item without naming it. $? reflects success of the most recent statement (true unless an error terminated or a native command returned nonzero). Other automatic variables include $PSVersionTable, $args, $PSCmdlet, and $Error.
bestPractices: Prefer $PSItem in shared scripts for readability
Check $? immediately after the command it refers to
commonMistakes: Reading $? after other statements have reset it
interviewTips: Mention $PSItem is the same as $_

title: Variable scopes in PowerShell
question: How do variable scopes work in PowerShell?
answer: PowerShell uses scopes such as Global, Script, Local, and Private, arranged hierarchically. Child scopes can read parent variables but assignments create a new variable in the local scope unless a scope modifier like $script: or $global: is used. This prevents functions from accidentally clobbering caller variables.
explanation: Scope modifiers (global:, script:, local:, private:) explicitly target a scope. The Script scope refers to the running .ps1 or module; Global is the session. Using scope modifiers carelessly can create hidden coupling, so prefer passing parameters and returning values.
codeExample:
$count = 0

function Add-One {
    $count = 5          # local copy, does not affect caller
    $script:count += 1  # explicitly modifies script-scope variable
}

Add-One
Write-Output $count   # 1, not 5
bestPractices: Prefer parameters and return values over global state
Use scope modifiers only when necessary
commonMistakes: Assuming a function can mutate a caller variable without a scope modifier
interviewTips: Explain read-up but write-local behavior

title: Splatting parameters
question: What is splatting and when should you use it?
answer: Splatting passes a collection of parameters to a command using a hashtable (or array) with the @ sigil instead of the $ sigil. It improves readability for commands with many parameters and lets you build parameter sets dynamically at runtime.
explanation: A hashtable maps parameter names to values, and @hash expands them onto the command. Array splatting supplies positional arguments. Splatting is ideal for long New-Item, Invoke-RestMethod, or Send-MailMessage calls and for conditionally including parameters.
bestPractices: Use splatting for commands with many parameters
Build hashtables to include parameters conditionally
commonMistakes: Using $hash instead of @hash when splatting
interviewTips: Note @ sign is the splat operator, not $

title: Comparison operators
question: How do the comparison operators -eq, -like, and -match differ?
answer: -eq performs an exact equality comparison, -like uses wildcard matching with * and ?, and -match uses regular expressions. All three are case-insensitive by default, with case-sensitive variants prefixed by c (-ceq, -clike, -cmatch).
explanation: When the left operand is a collection, comparison operators act as filters returning matching elements. -match also populates the $Matches automatic variable with capture groups. Choosing the right operator avoids brittle string logic.
codeExample:
'PowerShell' -eq 'powershell'      # True (case-insensitive)
'report.log' -like '*.log'         # True (wildcard)
'user123' -match '^user\d+$'       # True (regex)
$Matches                            # capture details from -match

# Collection filtering
1,2,3,4 -gt 2                       # 3, 4
bestPractices: Use -like for wildcards and -match for regex
Use c-prefixed operators when case matters
commonMistakes: Using -eq expecting wildcard behavior
interviewTips: Mention $Matches populated by -match

title: Error handling with try/catch
question: How does try/catch/finally error handling work in PowerShell?
answer: try/catch handles terminating errors, with catch running when an exception is thrown and finally always executing for cleanup. Non-terminating errors are not caught unless you set -ErrorAction Stop or the $ErrorActionPreference to Stop to promote them to terminating errors.
explanation: Most cmdlets emit non-terminating errors that stream to the error pipeline and continue execution. To catch them you must convert them to terminating errors. You can also catch specific exception types by naming them in the catch block for granular handling.
bestPractices: Use -ErrorAction Stop to catch cmdlet errors
Catch specific exception types before a generic catch
commonMistakes: Expecting catch to trigger on non-terminating errors
interviewTips: Explain terminating vs non-terminating errors clearly

title: Terminating vs non-terminating errors
question: What is the difference between terminating and non-terminating errors, and how does $ErrorActionPreference affect them?
answer: Terminating errors stop the current command or pipeline and can be caught by try/catch, while non-terminating errors report a problem but let execution continue. $ErrorActionPreference sets the default action for non-terminating errors session-wide, and -ErrorAction overrides it per command.
explanation: Common values are Continue (default), Stop, SilentlyContinue, and Ignore. Setting Stop converts non-terminating errors into terminating ones so try/catch can handle them. Errors are also recorded in the $Error collection regardless of the action preference.
bestPractices: Set -ErrorAction per command rather than globally when possible
Inspect $Error for recent failures
commonMistakes: Leaving $ErrorActionPreference at Stop globally and masking later issues
interviewTips: List the common ErrorAction values

title: Hashtables and PSCustomObject
question: When would you use a hashtable versus a PSCustomObject?
answer: A hashtable is an unordered key/value collection ideal for lookups, splatting, and configuration. A PSCustomObject is an ordered structured object with named properties that behaves like a typed record, making it the right choice for producing pipeline output that other cmdlets can consume.
explanation: Hashtables use @{} and are great for fast key access. Casting a hashtable to [PSCustomObject] produces an object whose keys become properties, preserving order. PSCustomObjects export cleanly to CSV, JSON, and Format-Table because they expose real properties.
codeExample:
$config = @{ Retries = 3; Timeout = 30 }   # hashtable lookup
$config['Retries']

$record = [PSCustomObject]@{
    Name   = 'web01'
    Status = 'Running'
    CPU    = 12.5
}
$record | Export-Csv .\report.csv -NoTypeInformation
bestPractices: Emit PSCustomObject for structured pipeline output
Use hashtables for lookups and splatting
commonMistakes: Outputting hashtables to the pipeline expecting property columns
interviewTips: Note [PSCustomObject] preserves key order

title: Advanced functions with CmdletBinding
question: What does [CmdletBinding()] add to a PowerShell function?
answer: [CmdletBinding()] turns a regular function into an advanced function that behaves like a compiled cmdlet. It enables common parameters such as -Verbose, -Debug, -ErrorAction, and -WhatIf, and gives access to $PSCmdlet for richer behavior like pipeline processing and ShouldProcess.
explanation: Advanced functions support parameter attributes like [Parameter(Mandatory)], validation attributes, and Begin/Process/End blocks for streaming pipeline input. This makes user-authored functions first-class citizens indistinguishable from built-in cmdlets.
codeExample:
function Get-LargeFile {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory)]
        [string]$Path,
        [int]$MinBytes = 1MB
    )
    Write-Verbose "Scanning $Path"
    Get-ChildItem -Path $Path -File |
        Where-Object Length -gt $MinBytes
}

Get-LargeFile -Path C:\logs -Verbose
bestPractices: Add [CmdletBinding()] to gain common parameters
Use Write-Verbose for optional diagnostics
commonMistakes: Reimplementing -Verbose logic instead of using CmdletBinding
interviewTips: Mention $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess for -WhatIf support

title: Pipeline input with ValueFromPipeline
question: How do you make a function accept pipeline input?
answer: Decorate a parameter with [Parameter(ValueFromPipeline)] or [Parameter(ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName)], then process incoming objects inside a process block. The process block runs once per piped object, enabling true streaming instead of collecting everything first.
explanation: ValueFromPipeline binds the whole incoming object to the parameter, while ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName binds a matching property. The begin block runs once for setup, process runs per item, and end runs once for finalization. This is how cmdlets handle large streams efficiently.
bestPractices: Put per-item logic in the process block
Use ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName for object inputs
commonMistakes: Placing pipeline logic in the param or begin block
interviewTips: Explain begin/process/end lifecycle

title: Parameter binding and validation
question: How does PowerShell parameter binding work and how do you validate input?
answer: PowerShell binds arguments to parameters by name, position, or pipeline, coercing types automatically. You constrain input with validation attributes like [ValidateSet], [ValidateRange], [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty], and [ValidatePattern], which reject bad values before the function body runs.
explanation: The binder resolves parameter sets, defaults, and mandatory prompts. Validation attributes execute during binding, providing clear errors at the boundary. Combined with typed parameters, this makes functions robust without manual if-checks inside the body.
codeExample:
function Set-LogLevel {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [ValidateSet('Info', 'Warn', 'Error')]
        [string]$Level,

        [ValidateRange(1, 90)]
        [int]$RetentionDays
    )
    "Level=$Level Retention=$RetentionDays"
}

Set-LogLevel -Level Warn -RetentionDays 30
bestPractices: Validate at the parameter boundary with attributes
Use [ValidateSet] to constrain to known values
commonMistakes: Validating inside the body instead of using attributes
interviewTips: Name several validation attributes to show breadth

title: String manipulation
question: What are common ways to manipulate strings in PowerShell?
answer: PowerShell supports .NET string methods (Split, Replace, Trim, Substring), the -replace regex operator, -split and -join operators, and string formatting with -f or subexpression interpolation. Double-quoted strings expand variables and subexpressions while single-quoted strings are literal.
explanation: The -replace operator uses regex, unlike the .Replace() method which is literal. -split can take a regex delimiter and a limit. The format operator -f applies .NET composite formatting for padding and number formatting. Subexpressions $() let you embed expressions inside interpolated strings.
codeExample:
$name = 'World'
"Hello $name, sum is $(2 + 3)"        # interpolation
'2026-01-05' -replace '-', '/'          # regex replace
'a,b,c' -split ','                       # split to array
'{0,-10}: {1:N2}' -f 'CPU', 12.3456     # formatted output
bestPractices: Use -replace for regex and .Replace() for literal swaps
Prefer -f for aligned, formatted output
commonMistakes: Expecting single-quoted strings to expand variables
interviewTips: Contrast -replace (regex) with .Replace (literal)

title: Here-strings for multi-line text
question: What is a here-string and when is it useful?
answer: A here-string is a multi-line string literal delimited by @" ... "@ (expandable) or @' ... '@ (literal). It preserves line breaks and quotes without escaping, making it ideal for embedding blocks of text such as JSON, SQL, HTML, or configuration templates.
explanation: The opening delimiter must be at the end of its line and the closing delimiter must be at the start of its own line with no leading whitespace. Double-quoted here-strings expand variables; single-quoted ones stay literal, which is safer for content containing $ characters.
codeExample:
$json = @"
{
    "name": "$env:COMPUTERNAME",
    "role": "web"
}
"@

$literal = @'
No $variable expansion happens here.
Use for templates with $ signs.
'@

$json | Out-File .\host.json
bestPractices: Use single-quoted here-strings for literal templates
Keep the closing delimiter flush to the left margin
commonMistakes: Indenting the closing @'@ or "@ delimiter
interviewTips: Note @"..."@ expands and @'...'@ is literal

title: Modules and Import-Module
question: How do PowerShell modules work and how are they loaded?
answer: A module is a reusable package of cmdlets, functions, and variables, typically a .psm1 script module with a .psd1 manifest. Modules on $env:PSModulePath auto-load when a command is called, or you can load them explicitly with Import-Module. Get-Module lists loaded modules.
explanation: The manifest (.psd1) declares metadata, version, dependencies, and exported members via FunctionsToExport. Auto-loading looks up commands in modules on the module path. Install-Module from the PowerShell Gallery downloads modules, and Export-ModuleMember controls what a script module exposes.
codeExample:
Get-Module -ListAvailable | Select-Object Name, Version

Import-Module Pester -MinimumVersion 5.0

# Inside MyTools.psm1
function Get-Widget { 'widget' }
Export-ModuleMember -Function Get-Widget
bestPractices: Ship a manifest and export only intended members
Rely on module auto-loading via PSModulePath
commonMistakes: Exporting all functions instead of a curated public surface
interviewTips: Distinguish .psm1 script module from .psd1 manifest

title: Execution policy
question: What is the PowerShell execution policy and is it a security boundary?
answer: Execution policy is a safety feature that controls whether and how scripts run, with values like Restricted, RemoteSigned, AllSigned, and Unrestricted. It is not a security boundary; it only reduces accidental script execution and can be bypassed easily, so it should not be relied on to stop determined attackers.
explanation: RemoteSigned is common: local scripts run, but downloaded scripts must be signed. Policies apply per scope (MachinePolicy, CurrentUser, Process, etc.). Because a user can pass -ExecutionPolicy Bypass or paste commands directly, it is a guardrail, not enforcement.
codeExample:
Get-ExecutionPolicy -List

Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope CurrentUser -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned

# One-off bypass for a single process
powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File .\deploy.ps1
bestPractices: Use RemoteSigned for a reasonable default
Set policy per-scope rather than machine-wide
commonMistakes: Treating execution policy as a real security control
interviewTips: Emphasize it is a guardrail, not a boundary

title: PowerShell remoting with Invoke-Command
question: How does PowerShell remoting work with Invoke-Command?
answer: Invoke-Command runs script blocks on one or many remote computers over WS-Management (WinRM), returning deserialized objects tagged with the source computer. It can target machines with -ComputerName, reuse persistent -Session objects, and fan out in parallel across many hosts.
explanation: Remoting uses PSSessions; results are serialized to XML and rehydrated locally, so they are property snapshots without live methods. Persistent sessions (New-PSSession) avoid repeated setup cost, and -ThrottleLimit controls concurrency for large fleets.
bestPractices: Reuse PSSessions for multiple remote calls
Tune -ThrottleLimit for large fleets
commonMistakes: Expecting live methods on deserialized remote objects
interviewTips: Mention objects are serialized/deserialized over WinRM

title: Providers, PSDrives, and format cmdlets
question: What are PowerShell providers and PSDrives, and why should Format-* cmdlets come last?
answer: Providers expose data stores (file system, registry, certificates, environment) through a uniform drive interface, so you navigate the registry or env vars with the same cmdlets as the file system. Format-* cmdlets (Format-Table, Format-List) produce display-only formatting objects, so they must be the final stage of a pipeline and never feed further processing.
explanation: PSDrives like C:, HKLM:, Cert:, and Env: are backed by providers you list with Get-PSProvider. Format cmdlets emit formatting instruction objects, not data, so piping them into Export-Csv or Where-Object breaks. Use Select-Object or calculated properties for data shaping, and Format-* only for on-screen presentation.
codeExample:
Get-PSProvider | Select-Object Name, Drives

# Navigate the registry like a drive
Get-ChildItem HKLM:\SOFTWARE | Select-Object -First 3

# Formatting must be terminal
Get-Process |
    Select-Object Name, Id, CPU |
    Format-Table -AutoSize   # do NOT pipe further
bestPractices: Use Select-Object for data and Format-* only for display
Explore registry and certs via their PSDrives
commonMistakes: Piping Format-Table output into Export-Csv or Where-Object
interviewTips: Stress that Format-* objects are display-only
